nodes.note: Lexical licensing condition: the verb must independently license intransitive motion (Goldberg 1995)
Actual or intended transfer of possession: 'She gave him a book' (Goldberg 1995: 38)
Caused enablement of transfer: 'She baked him a cake' (Goldberg 1995: 39)
Intended future transfer via promise: 'She promised him a car' (Goldberg 1995: 39)
Refusal of transfer: 'She refused him a dance' (Goldberg 1995: 40)

edges.notes: Ditransitive extends transitive by adding a Recipient argument; encodes intended or actual transfer (Goldberg 1995)
Caused motion extends transitive by adding a directional PP; encodes the Theme's change of location as constructionally entailed (Goldberg 1995)
Resultative extends transitive by adding a result XP encoding the end-state of the object (Goldberg 1995)
Passive shares the semantics of transitive but differs in information structure: the undergoer is topicalized and the actor is demoted or omitted (Goldberg 2005)
Way construction is a subtype of caused motion in which Agent and Theme are coreferential; encodes self-propelled motion to a goal by means of the action denoted by V (Goldberg 1995)
The result XP of the resultative can be interpreted as a metaphorical Goal via the CHANGE IS MOTION conceptual metaphor, linking resultative to caused motion (Goldberg 1995)
Time-away shares the NP V NP.poss X structure with the way construction but encodes temporal expenditure rather than spatial motion (Jackendoff 1997)
Both are fixed pragmatic interrogatives encoding irony/surprise (Kay & Fillmore 1999)
Time-away encodes result (temporal expenditure) and is arguably closer to resultative than to way_cxn (Goldberg 1995)

Each correlative clause in the the-X-the-Y schema may independently instantiate a transitive (or intransitive) argument structure; the construction is thus a complex clause-combining schema whose internal clauses are subparts governed by argument structure constructions (Fillmore, Kay & O'Connor 1988; Culicover & Jackendoff 1999)

The binominal NP (e.g. 'a bear of a man') is NP-internal but typically occupies an argument slot of a transitive or intransitive construction; the host clause is a subpart of the larger constructional environment (Lambrecht 1984; Kay & Michaelis 2012)

The WXDY schema 'What is NP doing VP?' embeds a transitive or intransitive clause as its core propositional subpart; the constructional meaning arises from the mismatch between the embedded clause and its pragmatic framing (Kay & Fillmore 1999)

The so-called 'intransitive resultative' (e.g. 'She ran herself ragged') contains a reflexive subpart that patterns with intransitive motion; the intransitive_cxn is thus a subpart of the broader resultative frame (Goldberg & Jackendoff 2004)
Central sense; prototypical transfer of possession (Goldberg 1995: 38)
Extension from actual to caused-enablement reading via metaphorical transfer (Goldberg 1995: 39)
Extension to intended future transfer; speaker commits to possession change (Goldberg 1995: 40)
Extends transfer by negating it; possession change is withheld (Goldberg 1995: 40)
